primer.markdown:
# Install, restart, verify — then register one tool

A working MCP server is a three-step loop, and the meta-prompting-plugin's `docs/QUICKSTART.md` shows the shape of that loop cleanly for any Claude Code extension: clone, run `INSTALL.sh`, restart Claude Code, then verify with a single command. An MCP server follows exactly that rhythm — you scaffold, you wire it into the client, you restart, and you send one request to confirm the handshake succeeded.

The TypeScript scaffold is three concrete moves. First, construct a `Server` with an `{ capabilities: { tools: {} } }` declaration (Module 02 already taught you why). Second, register a `ListToolsRequestSchema` handler that returns one `analyze` tool with a `zod`-compatible `inputSchema`. Third, register a `CallToolRequestSchema` handler that pattern-matches on the tool name and returns `content: [{ type: "text", text: ... }]`. Bind a `StdioServerTransport`, call `server.connect(transport)`, and you are done — that is a complete, shippable server in roughly thirty lines.

The QUICKSTART verification step translates directly: you wire the server into Claude Code's `~/.claude.json`, restart the client, and run a single test command (for a plugin that is `/meta --help`; for an MCP server it is whatever tool you registered — in the skill's example, `analyze`). If the client lists your tool, your `initialize` handshake worked, your capability bag was accepted, and your `ListToolsRequestSchema` handler fired. Three concentric contracts — transport, handshake, schema — all passed in a single round-trip.
primer.key_insight: Every MCP server ships in the same three-step loop: install / restart / verify. If `listTools` returns your registered tool, all three protocol layers are working.

visual.alt: A loop diagram with three labelled stations: `INSTALL` (a terminal icon running `./INSTALL.sh` and writing to `~/.claude.json`), `RESTART` (a Claude Code window icon cycling), and `VERIFY` (a terminal icon showing a single successful `tools/list` response with one tool entry). Curved arrows connect the three stations into a closed loop; a fourth arrow from `VERIFY` back to `INSTALL` is dashed and labelled `iterate — add more tools`.

sandbox_policy.csp: default-src 'none'; style-src 'unsafe-inline'; script-src 'unsafe-inline'; img-src data:; font-src https://fonts.gstatic.com; connect-src 'none'; frame-ancestors 'self'

interactive.success_criterion: Learner steps through a simulated server build: click `init` to scaffold, `register tool` to add a tool handler, `connect stdio` to bind the transport, then `verify` to simulate a `tools/list` round-trip. The success pill appears once the four buttons have been clicked in order and the simulator returns a populated tools array.

artifact.excerpt:
# Quickstart

Get productive in 5 minutes.

## Install

```bash
git clone https://github.com/manutej/meta-prompting-plugin ~/.claude/plugins/meta-prompting
cd ~/.claude/plugins/meta-prompting
./INSTALL.sh
```

Restart Claude Code.

## Verify

```
/meta --help
```

You should see the command frontmatter. If not, check that `~/.claude/commands/meta.md` is a symlink pointing into `~/.claude/plugins/meta-prompting/commands/meta.md`.

questions.prompt: `docs/QUICKSTART.md` gives a three-step install loop for any Claude Code extension. Which step comes immediately after running the installer?
questions.explanation: QUICKSTART.md line 13: `Restart Claude Code.` The client only re-reads its extension registry on startup, so install-then-verify without a restart in the middle will fail every time — for plugins and for MCP servers alike.

questions.prompt: In the `mcp-categorical` TypeScript scaffold, which handler does the client invoke during the `initialize` verification round-trip to enumerate available tools?
questions.explanation: `ListToolsRequestSchema`. The mcp-categorical SKILL.md registers it at lines 37-52; the client calls `tools/list` once the handshake completes, and the handler returns the tool array. `CallToolRequestSchema` is only invoked later, when a specific tool is executed.

questions.prompt: The `analyze` tool in the skill's example returns its output as `content: [{ type: "text", text: "Analyzed: ..." }]`. Why the `content` array rather than a bare string?
questions.explanation: MCP tool results are a list of typed content parts (`text`, `image`, `resource`, etc.), not a single string, so clients can render mixed-media output. A bare string would collapse that structure and break any tool that wants to attach, say, an image or a resource reference alongside its text answer.
